c74object.name: tap.autowah~
c74object.digest: Envelope filter (auto-wah) in the Snow White style
c74object.description: The tap.autowah~ object is an envelope filter modeled on the Mad Professor Snow White AutoWah pedal (a Björn Juhl design): a resonant 2-pole filter whose frequency is pushed up from the bias resting point by your playing dynamics, then falls back at the decay rate. The attack is fast — the pedal's signature — so every picked note articulates; a fast decay gives a wah on every note, a slow one gives classic auto-wah swells. The sweep is voiced like a real wah (a throaty resonant peak riding 250 Hz to 2.5 kHz by default — straight through vowel-formant territory), not the quack of a Mu-Tron-style filter. Sensitivity sets how hard your signal drives the sweep; at minimum the envelope is off and the object becomes the pedal's secondary mode — a fixed, manually swept "cocked wah" with bias as the sweep control. Extensions beyond the hardware, all neutral at their defaults: a dry/wet mix (the pedal is wet-only), a downward sweep direction, a drive stage into a saturating filter circuit (2x oversampled), a bandpass tap (the circuit's other output, a known hardware mod), and an adjustable attack. A signal in the right inlet sidechains the envelope detector so one signal can wah another; the right outlet emits the envelope (0..1) as a signal for patching elsewhere. All parameters glide smoothly, and sixteen preset slots can be stored and morphed between — slots 1-4 ship factory voicings (guitar, bass, slow swell, cocked wah). Single-channel: wrap in mc. for multichannel (each channel gets its own envelope).

method.name: store
method.description: Store the current parameters in a preset slot (1-16).

method.name: recall
method.description: Morph all parameters to a stored preset (1-16; slots 1-4 ship factory voicings: guitar, bass, slow swell, cocked wah). The optional second argument overrides the morph time in milliseconds (default: the interp attribute).

method.name: clear
method.description: Reset the filter state and the envelope.

attribute.name: sensitivity
attribute.description: Envelope-detector input gain in dB (-60..24) — the filter trigger level; tune it to your instrument's output and your touch. At -60 the envelope is off and the object becomes a fixed, manually swept filter with bias as the sweep control (the pedal's cocked-wah mode).

attribute.name: decay
attribute.description: How fast the filter falls back to the bias resting point, in milliseconds (10-5000). Fast gives a wah on every note; slow gives traditional auto-wah swells.

attribute.name: bias
attribute.description: The filter's resting frequency in Hz (20-20000) — where the sweep starts from and returns to. The hardware rests around 250 Hz.

attribute.name: resonance
attribute.description: Filter resonance / Q, normalized 0-1 (1 is the edge of self-oscillation).

attribute.name: range
attribute.description: Sweep span in octaves from the bias frequency (0-5). The hardware sweeps about 3.3 octaves (250 Hz to ~2500 Hz).

attribute.name: direction
attribute.description: Sweep direction: 0 = up from bias (the pedal), 1 = down from bias (a TapTools extension).

attribute.name: attack
attribute.description: Envelope attack in milliseconds (0.05-100). The hardware's attack is fast and fixed (about 2 ms, the default) — raise it for softer filter onsets.

attribute.name: mode
attribute.description: Filter tap: 0 = lowpass (the stock resonant voicing), 1 = bandpass (the circuit's other tap — quackier and noticeably quieter, a known hardware mod).

attribute.name: drive
attribute.description: Drive in dB (0-24) into a saturating filter circuit (2x oversampled) — an OTA-flavored color stage. 0 runs the pure linear filter.

attribute.name: mix
attribute.description: Dry/wet mix in percent, equal-power (0-100). The hardware is wet-only: 100, the default.

attribute.name: interp
attribute.description: Default preset-morph time in milliseconds used by the recall message.

attribute.name: smooth
attribute.description: Anti-zipper interpolation time in milliseconds applied to parameter changes.
